items.note: Three uses: (1) ONLY/NOTHING BUT: 「甘いものばかり食べています」 = I eat nothing but sweets. (2) QUANTITY ESTIMATION: 「30分ばかり待った」 = I waited about 30 minutes. (3) JUST-DONE → see 〜たばかり below. When used after a noun or verb dictionary form, it means 'only/nothing but'. 「勉強ばかりしていて、遊ぶ時間がない」 = All I do is study, there's no time to play. Different from だけ (only — neutral): ばかり has a nuance of 'too much / excessively only X'.

Verb (た-form) + ばかり = something happened very recently, still fresh. 「日本に来たばかりです」 = I just arrived in Japan (still adjusting, everything is new). 「食べたばかりなのに、もうお腹が空いた」 = I just ate, but I'm already hungry again. Compare: 〜てしまった = unfortunately did (regret focus). たばかり = time focus (very recent). 「さっき始めたばかりです」 = We just started a moment ago. Use this to explain you're new to something.

Three key meanings: (1) DEPENDING ON: 「人によって、好みが違います」 = Tastes differ depending on the person. 「天気によって予定が変わります」 = Plans change depending on the weather. (2) PASSIVE AGENT (by ~): 「この曲はベートーベンによって作られた」 = This piece was composed by Beethoven. (3) MEANS: 「努力によって成功した」 = Succeeded through effort. In travel contexts, によって expresses 'it depends': 「店によって違います」 = It depends on the shop.

english: toward ~ / in response to ~ / in contrast to ~
items.note: Two main uses: (1) DIRECTION OF ACTION/ATTITUDE: 「先生の質問に対して、答えました」 = I answered in response to the teacher's question. 「お客様に対して、丁寧に話します」 = I speak politely toward customers. (2) CONTRAST: 「兄は背が高いのに対して、弟は低い」 = The older brother is tall, whereas the younger is short. 「日本が小さいのに対して、ロシアはとても大きい」 = Japan is small, whereas Russia is very large.

Verb (dict/negative) + ことにする = a deliberate personal decision. 「明日から、早起きすることにしました」 = I've decided to start waking up early from tomorrow. 「今年は日本語のN2を受けることにした」 = I decided to take the N2 exam this year. 「お酒を飲まないことにしています」 = I have decided not to drink alcohol (ongoing decision). Contrast: ことになる = external decision (see below). つもりです = intention. ことにする = the decision itself.

english: it has been decided that ~ / it turns out that ~
items.note: Verb (dict/negative) + ことになる = the outcome of an external decision, circumstance, or fate — not purely your own choice. 「来月、大阪に転勤することになりました」 = It has been decided that I'll be transferred to Osaka next month. 「飛行機が遅れて、1日遅くなることになった」 = Due to the flight delay, it turned out we'd arrive a day later. Compare: ことにする = you decided. ことになる = it was decided (by others, or by circumstances).

english: it doesn't mean that ~ / not necessarily
items.note: Plain form + わけではない = denying an implication or expectation. 「日本語を勉強したからといって、ペラペラになるわけではありません」 = Just because you study Japanese doesn't mean you'll become fluent. 「嫌いなわけではない、ただ疲れているだけです」 = It's not that I dislike it, I'm just tired. わけ = reason / meaning. This pattern corrects a misunderstanding or qualifies something. Polite way to soften: 〜というわけでもないんですが… = 'it's not exactly that, but...'.

Plain form + に違いない = strong conviction, near certainty, based on reasoning. 「あの人は日本人に違いない」 = That person must be Japanese. 「こんなに遅い時間に電話するなんて、急ぎの用事に違いない」 = Calling at such a late hour — it must be urgent business. Certainty scale: かもしれない (maybe, 50%) < でしょう (probably, 70%) < はずです (should be, logical) < に違いない (must be, near-certain). Use when you're almost sure but can't be 100%.

Three uses: (1) HEARSAY: 「田中さんが来るらしいですよ」 = Apparently Tanaka-san is coming. 「明日、雨らしい」 = Apparently it'll rain tomorrow (someone told you). (2) INFERENCE from evidence: 「彼は学生らしい」 = He seems to be a student. (3) 'TYPICAL OF': 「男らしい」 = manly / typical of a man. 「あの映画、日本語が難しいらしいよ」 = Apparently that film has hard Japanese. Compare with そうです②: 来るそうです (I was told) vs 来るらしい (apparently, more informal, from scattered info).

Based on direct observation or inference. 「彼は疲れているようです」 = He seems to be tired. 「外が騒がしいね。何かあったみたいだ」 = It's noisy outside. Seems like something happened. みたい = casual; よう = more formal/written. Contrast: らしい = from information/hearsay. ようだ/みたい = YOUR OWN observation/inference. 「雨が降ったようで、道が濡れている」 = It seems it rained — the road is wet. Connect pattern with 〜そうだ① (visual appearance, Adj/V stem).

english: in the state of ~ / leaving ~ as is
items.note: Verb (た-form / negative) + まま + state that CONTINUES unchanged. 「窓を開けたまま、出かけてしまった」 = I went out leaving the window open. 「服を着たまま寝た」 = I slept while still dressed. 「昔のまま、変わらない」 = Unchanged, just as it was before. Negative ないまま: 「薬を飲まないままにしてはいけない」 = You shouldn't leave it without taking medicine. The まま state persists — nothing is done to change it, intentionally or not.

Noun + として = in the role, capacity, or quality of something. 「研究者として、日本に来ました」 = I came to Japan as a researcher. 「友達として話す」 = Talking as a friend (not in another role). 「問題として考える」 = Consider it as a problem. 「エジンバラ大学の学生として、国際会議に参加した」 = I participated in the international conference as a student of Edinburgh University. Different from に/で (location/means) — として marks what role or capacity you ARE occupying.

Plain form + ものの = concessive — admits something is true, but the result is disappointing or unexpected. 「日本語を3年勉強したものの、まだ上手に話せません」 = Although I've studied Japanese for 3 years, I still can't speak it well. 「行くつもりだったものの、雨で行けなかった」 = Although I intended to go, I couldn't because of rain. More formal than でも or けれど. The ものの clause presents something expected to lead to a positive result — but it falls short.

english: if only ~ then ~ / all you need is ~
items.note: 〜さえ = 'even / if only'. Combined with ば conditional = the ONE minimum condition needed. 「お金さえあれば、世界中を旅できる」 = If only I had money, I could travel the world. 「天気さえよければ、外で食べましょう」 = If only the weather is good, let's eat outside. 「日本語さえ話せれば、もっと楽しめる」 = If you could only speak Japanese, you'd enjoy it much more. Implies: nothing else is lacking — just this one thing.

A は B ほど adjective ない = A is not as [adj] as B. 「東京は思ったほど高くなかった」 = Tokyo wasn't as expensive as I thought. 「この映画はあの映画ほど面白くない」 = This movie isn't as interesting as that one. ほど marks the DEGREE of comparison — 'to the degree of B'. Very common paired phrase: 「思ったほど〜ない」 = not as ~ as I expected. Also positive: 「これほど美しい場所はない」 = There is no place as beautiful as this (ほど without ない).
